course_description:
Criminal Law in context involves the study of the general principles of criminal responsibility and procedure and examines how specific offences are made, defined, prosecuted and defended. The course examines how the operation of different institutions and actors (public and private police, prosecution, courts, defendants, juries, corrections) shape the boundaries of the criminal law, focusing on the role of discretion and public interest/policy considerations.

Criminal Law in Contextﾠ begins with an overview of key principles of criminal law, focusing specifically on the concept of criminal responsibility and exploring how this manifests in the construction of criminal offences and defences. We then turn to an examination of specific crimes, such as offences against the person, property offences, drug offences, and public order offences. At each point, we examine the criminal law by applying key principles to specific cases, and critically analysingﾠthe tensions and limits of the criminal law in context. As we explore the material, we also pay attention to issues of law reform in order to critically assess how the criminal law might best respond to emerging crime problems challenging our modern world.ﾠ

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the BCCJ, BCCJ(Hons), BA and BA duals except the BA/LLB.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: Criminal Law in Contextﾠ aims to equip students with requisite skills and knowledge to understand how criminal law is constructed and applied,ﾠand to critically examine its operation in context.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and describe key principles underpinning the criminal law, particularly the concept of criminal responsibility.
Understand how the criminal law operates in relation to specific offences, defences, and contexts.
Analyse and critically evaluate the criminal law within specific situations, contexts, and crime types.
Apply effective legal research skills, such as analysis of case law, legislation, and secondary sources, to understand the application of the law and to support arguments re: law reform.
Demonstrate and apply skills required to communicate effectively within a legal and policy context.

assessment_details.title: Activity One
assessment_details.due_date: 26/03/2026 3:00 pm The Activity is due at the end of the Week 5 seminar.
assessment_details.task_description: During the Week 5 Seminar (i.e. Thursday 26 March 2026) , you will be expected to complete and submit Activity One. This Activity is designed to demonstrate your engagement with and understanding of content covered in weeks 1-4 of the course. To prepare for this Activity, you will be guided to engage with materials and discussions during the Week 1 - 4 seminars, and you may be prompted to conduct some additional research, activities, and/or readings on relevant topics throughout these weeks in order to complete the activity. A detailed task guide and marking criteria will be available in the Assessment folder on the course Blackboard site in Week 1. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. Guidance on this requirement will be provided in the task guide. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Activity One must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site. Instructions for submission will be provided in the Activity One task guidelines.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If a student is unable to attend the seminar in Week 5 due to illness or exceptional circumstances, they will need to apply for an extension. An extension for this assessment task will provide eligible student with an opportunity to complete a comparable task in an alternative timed session. Details of the alternative session will be communicated to relevant students.
Extension to Assessment Due Date
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply . Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time . Requests for extensions received after the application due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Decision-Maker Tables in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to determine whether to accept the request for consideration.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Seminars The seminar topics are: Criminal Law and Criminal Responsibility Understanding Offences Understanding Defences Extending Criminal Responsibility Reflecting on Criminal Responsibility - and completion of Activity One . Unlawful Killing Offences against the Person Sexual and Domestic Violence Offences Applying and Critiquing the Criminal Law - and completion of Activity Two Property Offences and Corporate/Institutional Crime Drug Offences and Public Order Offences Applying and Critiquing the Criminal Law - and completion of Activity Three Review of Course - Criminal Law in Context Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
